ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for smoked provola cheese tempura specifically. FOODON_03315116 (cheese food product) used as closest parent. The tempura preparation and prosciutto accompaniment are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Cheese) used as closest parent. No specific code exists for smoked provola tempura or the composite dish with prosciutto in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226760005 (Cheese) used as closest parent. No specific concept exists for smoked provola cheese tempura in SNOMED CT.

main_ingredients: tempura batter (wheat flour, egg, cold water)

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of smoked provola cheese tempura with aged prosciutto is approximately 200 g, comprising battered and deep-fried smoked provola cheese slices, thin slices of aged prosciutto crudo, fresh tomatoes, arugula, balsamic glaze, and honey.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Provolone cheese (FDC ID 173420), Prosciutto (FDC ID 168322), Tempura batter fried coating (FDC ID 172448), Arugula (FDC ID 169387), Tomatoes (FDC ID 170457), Balsamic vinegar (FDC ID 172237), Honey (FDC ID 169640)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry exists for smoked provola cheese tempura with aged prosciutto in any approved primary source. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the primary components. Estimated dish composition by weight (per 100 g of plated dish): smoked provola cheese 35 g, aged prosciutto 15 g, tempura batter (fried) 20 g, arugula 10 g, tomato 10 g, balsamic glaze 5 g, honey 3 g, frying oil absorbed 2 g. Macros calculated proportionally from USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods data for each component.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on tempura-battered fried foods and cheese-dominant mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for smoked provola cheese tempura with aged prosciutto. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous tempura-battered fried cheese dishes and mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate meals. Tempura batter contributes refined wheat flour (GI ~70 for plain wheat flour), but the high fat and protein content from provola cheese and prosciutto substantially blunts the glycemic response. Mixed dishes with dominant fat and protein content typically yield GI in the 40–55 range. GI of 45 (low category, ≤55) is a conservative estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for cheese-and-batter fried foods. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (45 × 27.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 12.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from aged prosciutto and smoked provola cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 680 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1360 mg per restaurant serving), which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; the combination of aged cured meat and smoked cheese makes sodium reduction difficult without reformulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour tempura batter contributing moderate glycemic carbohydrates, partially offset by high fat and protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in moderation; while the estimated GI is low (approximately 45) due to fat and protein blunting the glycemic response, the tempura batter contributes refined carbohydrates (~27 g per serving) and the high saturated fat content from cheese and prosciutto may contribute to insulin resistance with regular consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from smoked provola cheese and aged prosciutto, combined with deep-frying and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional indulgence only, as the combination of high saturated fat (~9.4 g per 100 g), elevated sodium (~680 mg per 100 g), cholesterol (~72 mg per 100 g), and deep-frying method collectively elevates cardiovascular risk; regular consumption is inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Smoked Provola Cheese Tempura with Aged Prosciutto is an Italian-inspired appetiser or starter dish featuring slices of smoked provola cheese (a semi-soft stretched-curd cheese from Southern Italy, typically from Campania) encased in a light Japanese-style tempura batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is served alongside thin slices of aged prosciutto crudo (dry-cured Italian ham), fresh tomatoes, peppery arugula (rocket), a drizzle of balsamic glaze, and a touch of honey. The result is a sophisticated interplay of textures and flavours: the crispy, airy tempura shell gives way to a molten, smoky, mildly tangy cheese interior, while the salty, umami-rich prosciutto provides a savoury counterpoint. The sweetness of honey and the acidity of balsamic glaze balance the richness of the fried cheese. This dish is a fusion of Italian charcuterie and cheese traditions with Japanese tempura technique, commonly found in contemporary Italian restaurants in Singapore such as Mingle at Funan. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (approximately 285 kcal per 100 g) with significant protein (16.8 g) from cheese and cured meat, high fat (18.2 g, predominantly saturated at 9.4 g), and moderate carbohydrates (13.5 g) from the tempura batter. Sodium content is high (~680 mg per 100 g) due to both the smoked cheese and aged prosciutto. The dish is a source of calcium (310 mg per 100 g) and vitamin B12 (0.85 mcg per 100 g). Clinically, it is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat load, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to refined carbohydrate content in the batter, though the low estimated GI (45) reflects fat-protein blunting of glycemic response.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for smoked provola cheese tempura with aged prosciutto exists in any approved primary nutritional database. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the individual components (smoked provola/provolone cheese, prosciutto, tempura batter, arugula, tomato, balsamic glaze, honey). GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable fried cheese and mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component data; no single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported or calculable with sufficient confidence from the composite estimation method used — specifically: monounsaturated_fat_g (5.6), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8), trans_fat_g (0.3), potassium_mg (195.0), calcium_mg (310.0), iron_mg (0.9), cholesterol_mg (72.0), vitamin_c_mg (4.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (95.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.12), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.22), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.12), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.85), folate_mcg_dfe (18.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.4), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (290.0), magnesium_mg (22.0), zinc_mg (1.8), selenium_mcg (12.0), water_g (46.5) — all are estimated from USDA component data and are provided as best-effort approximations. No sentinel -1 values are used in this record; all fields carry estimated numeric values. 2. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 3. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for a typical restaurant portion; actual restaurant portion at Mingle - Funan may vary. 4. The dish is not halal-certified as prosciutto crudo is pork-derived. 5. Multilingual local names are provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional) as the dish is served in Singapore; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is an Italian dish without established local-language names in those languages — the Chinese names are provided as Singapore is a Chinese-majority market and the dish appears on a Singapore restaurant menu. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × available carbohydrate per serving (27.0 g from 200 g × 13.5 g/100 g) / 100 = 12.15, rounded to 12.2.
